skill.name: grafana-dashboards
skill.description: Create and manage production Grafana dashboards for real-time visualization of system and application metrics. Use when building monitoring dashboards, visualizing metrics, or creating operational observability interfaces.
skill.summary: Design production Grafana dashboards for metrics, alerts, variables, and dashboard-as-code workflows.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, filesystem, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found documentation-only false positives. The file contains Markdown fences, Grafana JSON, PromQL, Terraform, and Ansible examples with no executable code or prompt injection. No evidence found of malware, data exfiltration, credential access, or command execution.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown fences parsed as shell execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fence delimiters and documentation examples. They do not execute commands or instruct the agent to run untrusted shell input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown fences or plain reference lines in one documentation file. No executable Ruby, shell command, or tool invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False positive: PromQL status matcher parsed as path traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal detection is the PromQL HTTP status regex for 5xx responses. It is query syntax inside a Grafana dashboard example, not a filesystem path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a Prometheus expression using status matching inside JSON. It contains no file read, file write, or path construction behavior.

low_findings.title: False positive: weak cryptography pattern in documentation text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur in descriptive Grafana dashboard text and examples. No hashing, encryption, authentication, or cryptographic API usage appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review found no cryptographic function names or security-sensitive algorithm choices. The affected lines are headings, descriptions, file paths, or documentation references.

low_findings.title: False positive: Terraform folder reference parsed as reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detection appears in a Terraform Grafana folder identifier. It does not collect host, network, user, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line references a Grafana folder resource ID in an infrastructure-as-code example. No reconnaissance command, system probe, or data collection workflow is present.

content.value_statement: Teams need dashboards that show service health without noisy or confusing panels. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design Grafana dashboards using proven observability patterns.
actual_capabilities: Designs Grafana dashboard layouts using RED and USE monitoring methods.
Provides PromQL examples for request rate, error rate, latency, CPU, and service status panels.
Shows dashboard variables for namespace and service filtering.
Documents alert configuration patterns for high error rate monitoring.
Covers Grafana provisioning with YAML, Terraform, and Ansible examples.
Suggests dashboard patterns for APIs, infrastructure, databases, and applications.
limitations: Does not connect to a live Grafana instance or import dashboards automatically.
Does not validate PromQL against your actual metric names or label schema.
Does not include complete referenced asset files in this package.
Requires users to adapt examples for their Grafana version and data sources.

use_cases.title: Create service health dashboards
use_cases.description: Build API dashboards with request rate, errors, latency, and alert panels.

use_cases.title: Standardize infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: Plan node, pod, CPU, memory, disk, and network panels for shared operations views.

use_cases.title: Document dashboard provisioning
use_cases.description: Prepare dashboard-as-code examples for Terraform, Ansible, and Grafana file provisioning.

prompt_templates.title: Basic API dashboard
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new service dashboard.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Grafana dashboard plan for an HTTP API. Include panels for request rate, error rate, and p95 latency.

prompt_templates.title: Infrastructure dashboard
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for node and workload monitoring.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an infrastructure Grafana dashboard for Kubernetes. Include CPU, memory, disk, network, pod count, and node status panels.

prompt_templates.title: SLO-focused dashboard
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for service reliability tracking.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Grafana dashboard for service SLO monitoring. Include RED metrics, burn-rate style views, variables, and alert thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Dashboard-as-code rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dashboard-as-code implementation plan for Grafana. Include provisioning files, Terraform resources, Ansible deployment steps, and review checks.

output_examples.input: Design a production API monitoring dashboard.
output: A top row with request rate, error rate, and p95 latency summary panels.
A trend section for service-level request volume and latency over time.
An alert section that flags sustained 5xx error rates above the chosen threshold.
A variable-driven dashboard filtered by namespace and service.
Panels for CPU, memory, disk I/O, network traffic, pod count, and node status.
Operational notes for consistent units, thresholds, and panel naming.

best_practices: Start with the most important user-facing signals before detailed resource panels.
Use variables for namespace, service, and environment filters.
Test dashboards across different time ranges before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not create dashboards with many unrelated metrics in one view.
Do not copy PromQL examples without matching your real metric labels.
Do not rely on colors alone to explain severity or service state.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy dashboards to Grafana?
faq.answer: No. It provides design and provisioning guidance, but users must apply changes with their own Grafana tooling.

faq.question: Does it require Prometheus?
faq.answer: The examples focus on Prometheus metrics and PromQL, but the design principles can support other Grafana data sources.

faq.question: Can it create alert rules?
faq.answer: It can suggest dashboard alert patterns and thresholds. Users should validate alert behavior in their Grafana environment.

faq.question: Is this useful for Kubernetes monitoring?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes namespace, service, pod, and node monitoring concepts that fit Kubernetes dashboards.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code for dashboard planning and documentation workflows.

faq.question: Does it include complete dashboard JSON assets?
faq.answer: The skill references dashboard assets, but the audited package contains only the main SKILL.md file.
